does: This paper introduces SAVE, a training-free framework that reduces object hallucination in Multimodal Large Language Models by using Sparse Autoencoders to identify and steer model activations toward visual understanding features. The method employs a binary object-presence question-answering probe to discover which SAE latent features most strongly correlate with accurate visual information processing, then applies activation steering along these identified features during inference. The approach achieves substantial improvements over existing training-free baselines, with 10 percentage point gains on CHAIR_S metric and consistent improvements on POPE and MMHal-Bench benchmarks across multiple models and layers.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in understanding and mitigating hallucination in vision-language models, addressing a well-documented problem where MLLMs generate plausible but incorrect objects due to language priors overwhelming visual signals. It extends prior research on sparse autoencoders as interpretability tools for decomposing neural network behavior, adapting this framework to identify task-relevant features in multimodal systems. The paper also connects to earlier work on activation steering and mechanistic interpretability approaches that manipulate model internals to improve behavior, combining these techniques with the specific challenge of visual grounding in hallucination mitigation.
enables: This research opens directions for deeper investigation of how vision and language information interact within multimodal models, potentially revealing other feature types beyond visual understanding that could be separately controlled. The training-free nature of the approach suggests a practical pathway for deploying hallucination-reduction techniques across diverse model architectures without expensive fine-tuning. Future work could extend SAE-based steering to other hallucination types beyond object generation, explore how visual understanding features evolve across model layers and scales, or develop methods to automatically discover and prioritize different feature categories for various downstream tasks, advancing the interpretability and reliability of multimodal systems.
